content.description: Past tenses do not always refer to past time. In some sentences and with some verbs or phrases we use a past tense to refer to the present or the future or to a general situation. These include sentences that contain:

rows: a second conditional
I'd book the next flight if I **had** more time off work.
*suppose / what if / imagine*

I know it's probably not going to happen, but ***suppose** / **imagine*** I **won** a round-the-world trip!
I know it's probably not going to happen, but ***what if*** I **won** a round-the-world trip?
*would rather* + you/he/she/we/they
I'd **rather you didn't plan** the entire itinerary without consulting me.
*it's (high/about) time*
**It's (high/about) time** we **booked** our summer adventure.
polite questions
**Did** you **want** me to send you the details for the guided tour?
*wish / if only*
**If only / I wish I had** a bigger travel budget this year.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description:
*Suppose*, *what if* and *imagine* can also be followed by a present tense.
A present tense indicates that the situation is more likely to actually happen.
A past tense indicates that the situation is less likely to actually happen.
items: ✓ What if you **are** offered a free holiday? (more likely to happen)
✓ What if you **were** offered a free holiday? (less likely to happen)

content.description: We use different structures with *wish / if only*, depending on exactly what we want to express.

rows: Expressing wishes about the present, future or generally
*wish / if only* + past simple/continuous
I **wish** I **was travelling** around Southeast Asia instead of being stuck at my desk!
Expressing wishes about the past
*wish / if only* + past perfect simple/continuous
**If only I'd booked** the train tickets before the prices went up.
Criticising other people or complaining about a situation now, in the future or generally
*wish / if only* + would
**If only** Marco **would** stop taking photos of every single meal!
Expressing hypothetical ability or permission now, in the future or generally
*wish / if only* + could
I **wish** I **could** speak Japanese when I visit Tokyo.
Expressing desires in a formal way
*wish* + full infinitive
I **wish to enquire** about your guided trekking tours.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: We do not usually say *If only / I wish I would* ...
✓ **If only / I wish I had** enough time to visit all the historical sites.
✓ **If only / I wish I could** stay longer in Rome.
✗ If only / I wish I **would** have enough time to visit all the historical sites.
To express desires about the future, we often use *hope*.
✓ I **hope** I **will** get a chance to travel back to Japan one day.
✓ I **hope to travel** back to Japan one day.
✗ I **wish** I **will** get a chance to travel back to Japan one day.

content.description: *Although*, *though* and *even though* are used to express contrast. *Even though* is more emphatic than *although* and *though*. *Though* is more informal than *although* and *even though*.

rows: *Although / Though / Even though* + subject + verb, subject + verb
**Although / Though / Even though** I packed light, my rucksack was still too heavy.
subject + verb, *although / though / even though* + subject + verb
My rucksack was still too heavy, **although / though / even though** I had packed light.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: *Though* can also come at the end of a sentence.
✓ I packed very little. My rucksack was still too heavy, *though*.

content.description: *In spite of* and *despite* are used to express contrast. They mean exactly the same thing and take the same grammatical structures:

rows: *in spite of / despite* + -ing form, subject + verb
**In spite of / Despite** packing light, my rucksack was still too heavy.
*in spite of / despite* + the fact (that) + subject + verb, subject + verb
**In spite of / Despite** the fact (that) I packed light, my rucksack was still too heavy.
*in spite of / despite* + noun, subject + verb
**In spite of / Despite** my careful packing, my rucksack was still too heavy.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: We can also put *in spite of / despite* in the middle of the sentence.
✓ My rucksack was still too heavy **in spite of / despite** packing light.
We can also use the perfect -ing form to show that the action happened before the result.
✓ My rucksack was still too heavy **in spite of / despite having** packed light.
With *in spite of / despite* + -ing form, the subject of the main verb must also carry out the action of the -ing verb.
✗ The airport was chaotic **in spite of / despite** arriving early. (= The airport arrived early!)

content.description: *However* and *nevertheless* are used to express contrast. *However* is formal. *Nevertheless* is even more formal.

examples: The mountain trail was extremely difficult to follow. **However / Nevertheless**, the team eventually reached the summit.
The mountain trail was extremely difficult to follow. The team did eventually reach the summit, **however / nevertheless**.
The mountain trail was extremely difficult to follow. The team did, **however / nevertheless**, eventually reach the summit.

content.description: *While* and *whereas* are used to contrast two different facts or ideas.

rows: *while / whereas* + subject + verb, subject + verb
**While / Whereas** backpackers usually stay in hostels, business travellers tend to prefer hotels.
subject + verb, *while / whereas* + subject + verb
Business travellers tend to prefer hotels, **while / whereas** backpackers usually stay in hostels.

sections.title: A: Unreal past forms
sections.instructions: Write the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: Don't you think it's time you (tell) ...... the travel agent about the problem with the booking?
What if we (rent) ...... a campervan and drove up the coast for the summer?
I'd rather people (send) ...... me messages than (phone) ...... me while I'm on holiday.
Suppose you (be going to) ...... explore any country in the world. Where would you choose?
It's time to (go) ...... - the bus to the airport leaves in twenty minutes.
If you (have) ...... unlimited funds, which continent would you want to explore first?
Nick would join us on the hiking trip if he (not have to) ...... attend the conference this weekend.
We'd rather you (not lend) ...... Dan the camping equipment again - he never returns things in good condition.
It's high time I (leave) ...... - my taxi to the station is already outside.
What if we (get) ...... completely lost in the jungle? What would we do then?

sections.title: B: Error correction (Wishes)
sections.instructions: Each of the words or phrases in bold is incorrect. Rewrite them correctly.

items.sentence: I wish I **know** how to read a topographic map properly.
'If only I **listened** to the guide,' said Emma regretfully. 'Now we've missed the tour entirely.'
I wish you **did** stop complaining about the hotel - it was the only one available!
If only Carlos **can** get time off to come on the expedition with us.
Do you wish you **went** with Sam to the Amazon next month?
Excuse me. I wish **speaking** with your head of reservations immediately.
Ryan wishes like anything that he **was driving** more carefully on those mountain roads that night.
I wish I **would feel** well enough to go on the snorkelling trip this afternoon.
I **wish** I'll get a window seat on the long-haul flight.
If only you **to listen** to what the experienced local guides advise!
It's about time we ...... planning the route for our expedition properly.
I'd really rather you ...... the children run so close to the edge of the cliff.
...... a particular destination in mind, or were you just looking for general travel advice?
I wish the travel company ...... that we're not trying to be difficult - the error was entirely their fault.
I'd have been able to catch my connecting flight if only ...... to the departure gate on time.
Don't you sometimes wish you ...... a bird and fly wherever you wanted in the world?
Suppose we ...... that a new overland route through Central Asia had just opened. Would you want to try it?
Do you wish ...... a formal complaint about the conditions at the campsite?
The charity was ...... enough donations to fund all three expeditions this year.
Don't you wish we ...... the scenic coastal road tomorrow rather than the motorway?

sections.title: D: Wish transformations
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences so that the meaning remains the same.

items.sentence: I hate it when you keep criticising every aspect of the travel arrangements.
items.answer: I wish you wouldn't criticise me all the time.

items.sentence: It's such a pity that Sam has no idea how I feel about him!
items.answer: If only Sam knew how I felt about him.

items.sentence: I'd absolutely love to be with you in Switzerland right now.
items.answer: I wish I were with you in Switzerland right now.

items.sentence: Frank regrets not accepting the position at the travel agency when it was offered.
items.answer: Frank wishes he had taken the job when he had the chance.

items.sentence: Wouldn't you love to be standing in the middle of Times Square at this very moment?
items.answer: Don't you wish we were standing in the middle of Times Square right now?

items.sentence: It's a real shame that I can't navigate as well as Steve does.
items.answer: I wish I could play chess as well as Steve.

items.sentence: If you had a million euros, what would you spend it on?
items.answer: Suppose you had a million euros, what would you do?

items.sentence: I'd prefer it if you didn't smoke anywhere inside the cabin.
items.answer: I'd rather you didn't smoke in here.

items.sentence: It's getting very late - the children really should be in bed by now.
items.answer: It's high time they went to bed.

items.sentence: Although **F** Marcus is afraid of flying, he travels abroad regularly for work.
Despite **D** being afraid of flying, Marcus travels abroad regularly for work.
Despite the **H** fact that Marcus is afraid of flying, he travels abroad regularly for work.
Marcus is afraid of flying, **I** although he travels abroad regularly for work.
Despite his fear of flying, **C** Marcus travels abroad regularly for work.
In spite **A** of being afraid of flying, Marcus travels abroad regularly for work.
Despite having **G** been afraid of flying all his life, Marcus travels abroad regularly for work.
Marcus is afraid of flying. However, he **B** does travel abroad regularly for work.
Marcus is afraid of flying. He does **E** travel abroad regularly for work.

sections.title: F: Key word transformation (Contrast)
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
Although she was exhausted, Anna kept on hiking towards the summit.
**fact** In ...... she was exhausted, Anna kept on hiking towards the summit.

Clara had been sure she would pass the wilderness survival assessment. She failed it, however.
**been** Despite ...... would pass the assessment, Clara failed it.

James wasn't awarded the expedition grant even though he had submitted an outstanding proposal.
**spite** James wasn't awarded the expedition grant ...... an outstanding proposal.

Planes can still take off and land but ferries have been cancelled due to the storm.
**whereas** Ferries have been cancelled, ...... able to take off and land.

Despite having got excellent references, Laura couldn't secure the travel grant she needed.
**even** Laura couldn't secure the travel grant ...... excellent references.

Even though she'd been robbed twice while travelling, Chloe still loved exploring new destinations.
**having** Chloe still loved exploring new destinations ...... twice while travelling.

It's an exciting adventure film despite the plot being a little hard to follow.
**though** It's an exciting adventure film, ...... a little hard to follow.

Despite searching everywhere, the hikers failed to find the trail marker.
**although** The hikers failed to find the trail marker ...... everywhere.

Dan really enjoyed the kayaking competition even though he was beaten.
**of** In ......, Dan really enjoyed the kayaking competition.

Despite their thorough investigation of the case, the safety team didn't manage to identify the cause.
**investigated** Despite ......, the safety team didn't manage to identify the cause.

sections.title: G: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: Find the extra word in each line. There are 8 extra words. Click on the extra words to mark them.
sections.passage: My first solo trek through the mountains last autumn was quite an experience! [[Well]] if only [[that]] I'd planned my route a bit more carefully from the start. Although [[being]] my guide Marco had warned me about the unpredictable weather conditions, I decided to attempt the hardest trail on day one. When I finally reached the exposed ridge, I could see [[that]] the storm was closing in rapidly. Despite [[of]] this obvious warning, I pressed on towards the summit regardless. If I could [[have]] manage this, I thought, I could manage anything. But even though [[that]] I had a detailed map with me, I somehow got completely lost. My head torch failed unexpectedly – even [[if]] though it had been fully charged that morning – and I had to feel my way along the path in pitch darkness. It was absolutely terrifying! [[Somehow]], despite the disastrous turn of events [[then]], a mountain rescue volunteer appeared on the trail and guided me safely back to the base camp. I was utterly relieved and grateful. The second trip was considerably better planned, I can assure you!

sections.title: H: Cloze (Contrast and wishes)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: (1) ...... the fact that I always try to stay positive in my travel blog, I have to admit that my recent trip to South America left me feeling deeply frustrated.
If only I (2) ...... somehow go back and plan the whole journey more carefully the second time around.
The scenery was absolutely breathtaking, I must say. (3) ......, the transport arrangements were a complete disaster from start to finish.
The roads were in an appalling condition, (4) ...... the views from the high mountain passes were simply stunning.
That's all in (5) ...... of the fact that the journey took nearly twice as long as expected, of course.
It really is (6) ...... time that the regional authorities invested properly in road infrastructure for travellers.
I know it's supposed to be part of the adventure, but suppose you actually (7) ...... the mistake of booking an overnight bus with no functioning air conditioning in midsummer.
You'd understand immediately why I wasn't saying that I wish I (8) ...... just stayed at a comfortable hotel by the beach instead.

But I do hope that my next expedition (9) ...... be far better organised, and then – (10) ...... though the roads remain rough and the buses unreliable – at least I'll still find the adventure that only travel can truly offer.
